solar_system.funFact: The Sun's core is 15 million degrees Celsius! Light takes 8 minutes to reach Earth.
The Moon is Earth's only natural satellite.
It is the smallest planet and closest to the Sun. It has almost no atmosphere.
The hottest planet in our solar system because of its thick greenhouse atmosphere.
Home to Olympus Mons, a volcano three times taller than Mount Everest.
A gas giant so massive that 1,300 Earths could fit inside it.
Its spectacular rings are made of billions of chunks of ice and rock.
An ice giant that tilts so far it actually orbits on its side.
Has the strongest winds in the solar system, reaching 2,100 km/h.
Pluto is a dwarf planet in the Kuiper Belt and needs 248 Earth years to orbit the Sun.

brightest_stars.funFact: The brightest star in the night sky. Known as the 'Dog Star'.
The second brightest star in the sky. Used by ancient sailors for navigation.
Our closest neighbor. Actually a triple star system.
An orange giant. Name means 'Guardian of the Bear' in Greek.
The first star ever to be photographed (in 1850).
A system of four stars. Known as the 'Little She-Goat'.
A blue supergiant marking the left foot of Orion the Hunter.
One of our closest neighbors. Name means 'before the dog'.
A red supergiant that may explode as a supernova soon.
The flattest star known due to its rapid rotation.
A blue-white giant. Also known as Agena, 'the knee'.
Spins so fast it is flattened at its poles.
The brightest star in the Southern Cross.
The giant red 'Eye of the Bull' in the constellation Taurus.
A red supergiant often confused with Mars. Name means 'Rival of Mars'.
A binary system of two stars so close they are egg-shaped.
The brighter of the Gemini twins. The 'immortal' twin in myth.
Surrounded by a massive disk of dust and debris.
One of the most distant stars visible to the naked eye.
A bright blue-white giant in the Southern Cross.
The 'Little King' of the Lion, located on the ecliptic.
Brightest known source of extreme ultraviolet light.
Actually a complex system of six individual stars.
A red giant and the top star of the Southern Cross.
A triple star system at the tip of the scorpion's tail.
A hot blue-white giant marking Orion's left shoulder.
Marks the tip of the bull's northern horn.
A bright star in Carina. Name means 'peaceful waters'.
The center star of Orion's belt. Name means 'string of pearls'.
The easternmost star of Orion's belt.
A bright blue-white subgiant. Name means 'the bright one'.
The brightest star in the Big Dipper.
Massive star system containing a rare Wolf-Rayet star.
A pointer star used to find the North Star.
The brightest star in the constellation Perseus.
A massive yellow-white supergiant in Canis Major.
The brightest star in the Archer's bow.
The star at the very tip of the Big Dipper's handle.
A binary system. Part of the 'False Cross'.
An eclipsing binary star system in Auriga.
A bright orange giant in the southern sky.
A white subgiant marking the foot of Gemini's Pollux.
A blue-white subgiant named after the bird it represents.
The North Star. Stays fixed while the sky rotates.
A blue-white giant that pulses in brightness.
The only bright star in Hydra. 'The solitary one'.
A beautiful binary system of two giant stars.
The brightest star in the constellation of the Ram.
An orange giant marking the sea monster's tail.
A hot blue-white star in the Teapot handle.
An orange giant. Name means 'shoulder of the centaur'.
Connects Andromeda and Pegasus. 'Navel of the horse'.
A red giant used to find the Andromeda Galaxy.
One of the two 'Guardians of the Pole'.
Marks the right foot of Orion the Hunter.
Brightest star in Ophiuchus, the Serpent Bearer.
The 'Demon Star' that blinks every few days.
A red giant star in the Crane constellation.
A blue-white subgiant marking the crane's tail.
A bright white star marking the lion's tail.
A yellow-white subgiant very close to Arcturus.
A white supergiant. Name means 'the little shield'.
An orange supergiant star in the sails of Vela.
The jewel in the Northern Crown (Corona Borealis).
The westernmost star in Orion's belt.
A supergiant at the center of Cygnus the Swan.
A bright star in the long river constellation Eridanus.
One of the most luminous stars in the Milky Way.
A stunning quadruple star system in Andromeda.
An orange supergiant marking the horse's nose.
A blue-white subgiant. Part of the 'False Cross'.
One of the stars in the Big Dipper's bowl.
A pointer star used to find Polaris.
A beautiful double star system in Boötes.
Famous for its nearby companion star, Alcor.
The brightest star in the Sea Goat.
A blue-white giant in Corvus the Raven.
An old red giant star marking the whale's nostril.
A blue supergiant marking the dog's tail.
A star in the Great Square of Pegasus.
A red giant that varies in brightness.
The only star that sometimes looks green to the eye.
The brightest star in the Serpent. 'Neck of the snake'.
A rapidly spinning star in Cepheus.
A binary system of two white stars in Ophiuchus.
A star marking the lion's paw in Leo.
A blue-white subgiant in the Dove constellation.
A wide double star system in Libra.
An orange giant in the swan's wing.
A bright star that heralds the rising of Sirius.
A bright variable star in the Gemini twins.
A bright yellow-white giant in the sails.
A bright binary star marking the 'end of the river'.
Marks the head of the water snake Hydra.
A star marking the water bearer's hand.
A yellow-white dwarf star near the ecliptic.
A star system located near the red giant Antares.
